item.title: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in…
item.description:
“Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in those forms, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then that monk is called one who has not swallowed Māra’s hook, who has broken the hook, destroyed the hook, who has not fallen into misfortune, into calamity, not subject to the Evil One’s will. There are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … phenomena cognizable by the mind … If a monk does not delight in those phenomena, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then that monk is called one who has not swallowed Māra’s hook, who has broken the hook, destroyed the hook, who has not fallen into misfortune, into calamity, not subject to the Evil One’s will.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.230
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Fear, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Suffering, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Disease, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A…
item.description:
“Fear, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Suffering, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Disease, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A boil, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A dart, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Bondage, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Mud, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A womb, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures.
How, monks, is fear a synonym for sensual pleasures? Because those who are infected by sensual desire, bound by sensual lust, are not freed from fear in the present life, not freed from fear in the future — therefore fear is a synonym for sensual pleasures.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 8.56
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Uttiya: “Hearing a sound, mindfulness is lost — Attending to a pleasing sign, The mind feels infatuation, Clinging to the perception remains. The defilements…
item.description:
Elder Uttiya:
“Hearing a sound, mindfulness is lost —
Attending to a pleasing sign,
The mind feels infatuation,
Clinging to the perception remains.
The defilements increase,
Leading to defilement in saṃsāra.”

From: Theragāthā 1.99
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, is the peril in the case of forms? Here, monks, one might see that woman at another time — eighty, ninety, or…
item.description:
“And what, monks, is the peril in the case of forms? Here, monks, one might see that woman at another time — eighty, ninety, or a hundred years old — aged, bent like a roof, crooked, leaning on a staff, trembling as she walks, sick, her youth exhausted, teeth broken, hair grey, bald, wrinkled, limbs shriveled and pale. What do you think, monks? Has her former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Thus, monks, is the peril in the case of forms.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 13 – Mahādukkhakkhandhasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what is a course of practice that is painful in the present but yields pleasant results in the future? Here, monks, a person by…
item.description:
“Monks, what is a course of practice that is painful in the present but yields pleasant results in the future? Here, monks, a person by nature has strong lust, and he constantly experiences painful and grievous feelings born of lust. By nature he has strong ill-will, and constantly experiences painful and grievous feelings born of ill-will. By nature he has strong delusion, and constantly experiences painful and grievous feelings born of delusion. With pain and grief, with tearful face and crying, he lives the holy life that is fully complete and pure. At the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a good destination, a heavenly world, in this realm. This, monks, is called a course of practice that is painful in the present but yields pleasant results in the future.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 45 – Cūḷadhammasamādānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Truly, brahmins, this world is led by aging, illness, and death. Since the world is led by aging, illness, and death, brahmins, restraint of body,…
item.description:
“Truly, brahmins, this world is led by aging, illness, and death. Since the world is led by aging, illness, and death, brahmins, restraint of body, speech, and mind in this life is a refuge, a cave, an island, a shelter, and a destination for one who has gone to death.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.51
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if a monk has a mind that is dispassionate and liberated regarding the form element, not grasping the defilements; regarding the feeling element ……
item.description:
“Monks, if a monk has a mind that is dispassionate and liberated regarding the form element, not grasping the defilements; regarding the feeling element … perception element … formation element … consciousness element, he has a mind that is dispassionate and liberated, not grasping the defilements. Being liberated, he is firmly established. Being firmly established, he is content. Being content, he is not agitated. Being not agitated, he personally attains Nibbāna. He knows: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.45
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “What is the cause, good sirs, what is the condition for the non-arising of unarisen greed, or for the abandonment of arisen greed?” - “The…
item.description:
“What is the cause, good sirs, what is the condition for the non-arising of unarisen greed, or for the abandonment of arisen greed?” - “The sign of the foul.” For one who attends wisely to the sign of the foul, unarisen greed does not arise and arisen greed is abandoned. This, good sirs, is the cause, this is the condition for the non-arising of unarisen greed and for the abandonment of arisen greed.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.68
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, suppose the Ganges river carries along a great pile of foam. A person with good eyesight would look at it, reflect, and examine it.…
item.description:
“Monks, suppose the Ganges river carries along a great pile of foam. A person with good eyesight would look at it, reflect, and examine it. Looking, reflecting, examining, the foam would appear empty, hollow, insubstantial. For what substance could there be in a pile of foam? So too, monks, whatever form there is, past, future, or present, internal or external, gross or subtle, inferior or superior, far or near — a monk sees form, reflects, examines it. Seeing, reflecting, examining, form appears empty, hollow, insubstantial. For what substance could there be in form?”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.95
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: O monks, these six sense bases — Where they are unrestrained, There suffering comes to be. Those who learn to guard them, To restrain and…
item.description:
O monks, these six sense bases —
Where they are unrestrained,
There suffering comes to be.
Those who learn to guard them,
To restrain and protect them,
With faith as their companion,
Dwell freed from sensual lust.

Seeing forms that are pleasing,
Seeing forms that are displeasing,
Restrain the path of lust
For forms that are pleasing —
Do not let the mind be defiled,
Thinking: ‘I do not delight in forms.’

Having heard sounds —
Pleasing and displeasing,
Do not be infatuated with pleasing sounds.
Restrain the mind’s aversion
For sounds that are displeasing,
Do not let the mind be defiled,
Thinking: ‘I do not delight in sounds.’

Having smelled fragrant, delightful odors,
Having smelled foul, loathsome odors —
Restrain aversion for loathsome odors,
And for delightful ones,
Do not let desire dominate.

Having tasted sweet flavors,
And also flavors that are not sweet,
Do not develop greed
When enjoying a sweet taste,
Do not utter words of opposition
When tasting what is not sweet.

When touched by pleasant contact,
Do not be infatuated with lust;
When touched by painful contact,
Do not be violently shaken.
For both contacts — pleasant and painful —
Remain equanimous,
Without approval or opposition,
Whatever contact may arise.

Those who follow proliferated perceptions,
Who are lost in conceptual proliferation,
Who act upon false thoughts —
Abandon all household activities
Born of the mind,
Practice restraint in your actions,
Directing yourself to the path of seclusion.

Thus, with the mind well-developed
Regarding these six sense bases,
Whatever contact occurs,
The mind is not disturbed.
Having subdued both greed and hatred,
O monk, go to the far shore,
Beyond the bonds of birth and death.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.94
📖 Read this sutta
